beats.text: A model can only learn relationships its inputs make expressible, so feature engineering changes the hypothesis class by changing the inputs.
The XOR pattern is perfectly determined yet inseparable by any line, since each class fills opposite quadrants of the plane.
Add the single product feature x1 times x2 and the problem collapses to one threshold a linear model solves exactly.

visual.note: Exact on XOR; raw linear cannot exceed 50%

beats.text: A degree-2 polynomial on two inputs adds three squared-plus-interaction terms: the squares and the single cross product.

visual.label: new degree-2 features for two inputs

beats.text: Term count grows combinatorially: a hundred inputs at degree two already yields thousands of features.
Engineered features are not free, so create generously in the lab and ship parsimoniously after hard pruning.

visual.note: Curvature is cheap; the dimensionality it buys is not

beats.text: Most real signal arrives as timestamps, free text, and one-to-many table relationships, each needing its own transform family.

visual.sub: Where domain knowledge pays off most

beats.text: A raw timestamp only expresses later, so the information lives in its components like hour, weekday, and month.
Cyclical times need a sine and cosine pair so hour 23 sits adjacent to hour 0 on a unit circle.

visual.note: One number cannot encode a cycle without a discontinuity; two can

beats.text: TF-IDF turns text into features by counting terms then down-weighting words common across many documents.

visual.note: Common words near zero; rare distinctive words weighted high

beats.text: Aggregation rolls many rows into one unit: counts, means, recency, and ratios over time windows for churn and fraud.
Aggregation and time features are the richest sources of leakage, so every window must use only data before the prediction moment.

visual.label: most common reason notebook models collapse on deploy

beats.text: Section 4.1 generates features by the hundred, and selection throws most of them away for less overfitting and readable models.
Filter methods score each feature against the target alone, blisteringly fast but blind to interactions and redundancy.
Recursive feature elimination fits the model, drops the lowest-importance feature, then refits, judging features in combination.

visual.note: O(p) fits versus 2^p for exhaustive search

beats.text: The Lasso adds an L1 penalty whose cornered geometry drives many coefficients to exactly zero during the fit.
As noise rises and signal falls, the univariate filter degrades first while model-aware RFE and L1 hold on longer.
Usefulness splits into two questions: importance about the target, and redundancy already told by the other features.
Correlation is cheap but sees only linear association, so a perfect quadratic relationship can have correlation zero.
Mutual information measures any statistical dependence, linear or not, as the bits a feature buys about the target.

visual.note: Zero if and only if X and Y are independent

beats.text: The variance inflation factor quantifies redundancy by how well the other features explain feature j.

visual.note: R_j squared from regressing x_j on all other features

beats.text: At an R-squared of 0.8 the variance inflation factor is exactly 5, the conventional warrants-a-look threshold.
Rules of thumb: a VIF above 5 warrants a look and above 10 signals serious collinearity in the coefficients.
The most expensive mistake in applied machine learning is selecting features on all data, then cross-validating only those.
With 10,000 features and 200 samples, picking the best on the full set lets test folds influence which features survive.

visual.label: features per sample in the leakage trap

beats.text: Selection is part of model fitting, so done on all data the biased estimate runs far above the honest one.

visual.note: Test-fold labels leaked into the choice

beats.text: On pure coin-flip labels the biased pipeline fakes accuracy well above chance while the honest one returns to one half.
The fix puts selection inside each fold, and when tuning too, nested cross-validation estimates the whole procedure.
The rule is absolute: anything you learn from the data is part of the model and must be cross-validated as a unit.
